home.title: Hi, I’m Berkay.
home.headline: Acoustics, Vibration & Signal Processing Engineer
home.intro: I develop measurement, signal-processing, and validation methods for audio, vehicle, rail, and sensor systems.

audio.title: Acoustics & Audio Product Development Engineer
audio.intro: I combine psychoacoustic evaluation, acoustic measurements, signal analysis, and mechanical-engineering experience to help teams design audio and mobility products that are measurable, usable, and perceptually effective.
evidence: Peer-reviewed AVAS research in the Journal of the Audio Engineering Society
International talks on warning-sound design and sound quality
Hands-on experience with microphones, binaural recording, Artemis SUITE, and Squadriga
audio.cta: Discuss an audio or acoustics role

test-validation.title: Test & Validation Engineer
test-validation.intro: I design controlled experiments, build measurement workflows, analyse multi-sensor data, and convert test results into engineering decisions across rail, automotive, medical-device, and sensor applications.
fit: Cross-functional mechanical, electronics, and software experience
evidence: Current rail-component condition-monitoring research at TH Wildau
Patented medical-device systems and industrial sensor work
Experimental projects spanning prototypes, measurements, statistics, and reporting
test-validation.cta: Discuss a test or validation role

signal-processing.title: Acoustic Signal Processing Engineer
signal-processing.intro: I work with acoustic and vibration data from experiment design through feature extraction, statistical modelling, and interpretation, with a focus on signals that must support product or diagnostic decisions.
fit: PCA, MANOVA, logistic regression, and hypothesis testing
evidence: Perceptual models connecting signal metrics to semantic attributes
Vibro-acoustic features for detecting loosened joints
Analysis of multi-condition listening and measurement datasets

projects.title: Electric-Truck Sound Design & Psychoacoustic Modelling
projects.subtitle: Turning perception into measurable product requirements
projects.problem: Electric trucks need warning sounds that are detectable and recognisable without sounding unnecessarily harsh or annoying.
projects.contribution: Designed two psychoacoustic study programmes, synthesised and evaluated vehicle sounds, analysed semantic and annoyance ratings, and translated perceptual responses into sound-design guidance.
projects.result: Delivered two listening-test datasets with 20 and 21 participants, including 24- and 35-stimulus evaluations; produced one peer-reviewed JAES article and four associated international conference contributions.
projects.relevance: Demonstrates the ability to convert subjective user perception into quantitative requirements for audio, mobility, and warning-system products.

projects.title: Vibro-Acoustic Condition Monitoring of Bolted Joints
projects.subtitle: Detecting looseness through acoustic and vibration signatures
projects.problem: Loss of bolt preload can alter stiffness, damping, resonances, and nonlinear response before visible failure occurs.
projects.contribution: Designed and executed experiments using microphones and accelerometers, varied tightening conditions, and evaluated preload-sensitive spectral and transfer-function features.
projects.result: Established preload-sensitive acoustic and vibration indicators using microphone and triaxial-accelerometer measurements; converted the work into two accepted/published 2026 conference contributions.
projects.relevance: Directly applicable to predictive maintenance, structural diagnostics, rail systems, machinery, and hardware reliability.

projects.title: Audibility & Masking in Electric-Truck Cabins
projects.subtitle: Designing warning signals for realistic noise environments
projects.problem: In-cabin warnings must remain detectable across operating conditions without becoming intrusive or fatiguing.
projects.contribution: Developed an adaptive listening-test procedure using binaural vehicle recordings and measured frequency-dependent masking thresholds under controlled conditions.
projects.result: Measured frequency-dependent audibility limits at six test frequencies (200 Hz to 6.4 kHz) across three electric-truck driving-speed conditions, providing design boundaries for in-cabin warning sounds.
projects.relevance: Relevant to automotive HMI, alarm design, occupational safety, and any product where sound must remain audible in noise.

projects.title: Flexible Piezoelectric Sensors & Energy Harvesters
projects.subtitle: Modelling, fabrication, and experimental validation
projects.problem: Flexible sensing and energy-harvesting devices require efficient models and robust experimental validation before integration into structural-health-monitoring systems.
projects.contribution: Designed, modelled, fabricated, and tested PVDF-TrFE devices and compared lumped-element and finite-element models for piezoelectric transducers.
projects.result: Delivered two peer-reviewed journal articles, one master’s thesis, and one IEEE conference contribution spanning flexible energy harvesters and miniaturised ultrasonic transducers.
projects.relevance: Shows cross-domain competence in sensors, transducers, simulation, prototyping, and measurement-driven product development.

projects.title: Medical IoT & Patented Monitoring Systems
projects.subtitle: Product concepts for safer hospital workflows
projects.problem: Hospital equipment and invasive devices require reliable monitoring and communication concepts to reduce unnoticed failures and workflow risks.
projects.contribution: Co-founded a medical-IoT start-up, developed sensing and device-communication concepts, and contributed to electronics and system design.
projects.result: Co-developed three protected medical-device concepts and secured a USD 25,000 TÜBİTAK techno-start-up grant for product development.
projects.relevance: Demonstrates product ownership, interdisciplinary development, intellectual property creation, and the ability to translate clinical needs into engineering concepts.

experience.title: Researcher
experience.company: Department of Machine Dynamics and Acoustics, TH Wildau
experience.project: SIAMIS — intelligent and automated mobile inspection of rail vehicles
bullets: Develop acoustic- and vibration-based condition-monitoring methods for loose bolted joints and structural defects in rail-vehicle components.
Design AI-supported diagnostic workflows for automatic fault detection, localisation, and severity estimation.
Lead development of a laboratory demonstrator integrating microphones, accelerometers, and acoustic-emission sensors for controlled excitation and validation tests.
Coordinate measurements on rail vehicles and collaborate with industrial and academic partners to transfer research outcomes into scalable maintenance solutions.

experience.title: Doctoral Researcher
experience.company: Chair of Acoustics and Haptics, TU Dresden
experience.project: Perception-based vibro-acoustic engineering for electric mobility and whole-body vibration
bullets: Conducted acoustic and vibration measurements for electric-truck Acoustic Vehicle Alerting Systems and developed binaural sound-reproduction workflows.
Designed psychoacoustic listening studies with 20–21 participants and up to 35 sound stimuli to assess sound quality, annoyance, urgency, masking, and perceptual discrimination.
Developed experimental methods for masking-threshold estimation and haptic perception, and analysed results using PCA, MANOVA, logistic regression, non-parametric tests, and psychoacoustic metrics.
Supervised senior engineering students in vehicle sound design and practical acoustic-measurement projects.

experience.title: Design Engineer
experience.company: TEI – TUSAŞ Engine Industries / GE Aviation Engines
experience.project: Mechanical design support for GE Aviation engine programmes at the Gebze Technology Centre
bullets: Supported design-engineering activities for GE Aviation engine programmes within a multidisciplinary aerospace environment.

experience.title: Co-Founder
experience.project: Government-funded medical-IoT systems for monitoring hospital devices and invasive equipment
bullets: Co-founded a technology start-up developing monitoring concepts for ventilation, infusion equipment, vascular-access dislodgement, and nasogastric-tube systems.
Contributed to sensing, device communication, electronic-system concepts, prototype development, and intellectual-property preparation.

experience.title: Flexible Electronics Engineer Intern
experience.project: Thin films and flexible electronic sensors
bullets: Supported experimental development and testing of flexible electronic sensor systems.
Developed LabVIEW software for repeatable laboratory testing.

experience.title: Graduate Researcher
experience.project: Flexible piezoelectric energy harvesters, PMUTs, and wearable sensing systems
bullets: Modelled, fabricated, and tested PVDF-TrFE energy harvesters and miniaturised ultrasound transducers using COMSOL FEM and acoustic equivalent circuits.
Extended earlier undergraduate work on wearable sensing for vascular-access dislodgement detection.

additionalExperience.title: Automotive R&D Intern
additionalExperience.detail: Developed a 22,000-line Visual Basic application that automated CATIA workflows and integrated more than 100 distinct 3D parts for R&D engineers.

additionalExperience.title: Mechanical Engineering Intern
additionalExperience.detail: Completed a long-term placement in the Safety Management Systems Department.

additionalExperience.title: Engineering Intern
additionalExperience.detail: Completed theoretical FEM training and practical PATRAN and CATIA assignments, documenting engineering solutions in weekly technical reports.

additionalExperience.title: Teaching Assistant
additionalExperience.detail: Supported the senior-level Mechanical Engineering Laboratory course.

service.title: T14 SS03: Sound Quality and Product Design

talks.title: Perceived Annoyance in Multi-Source Electric Vehicle AVAS Environments
talks.authors: Berkay Kullukçu, Serkan Atamer, Jonas Krautwurm, and M. Ercan Altinsoy
talks.conference: 32nd International Congress on Sound and Vibration (ICSV32)
talks.session: T14 SS03: Sound Quality and Product Design
talks.abstract: This study extends AVAS evaluation from isolated vehicle sounds to realistic multi-vehicle traffic scenes. Binaurally rendered single- and two-vehicle pass-by scenes were assessed in a listening test using perceived-annoyance ratings. Simultaneous two-vehicle scenes produced a significant increase in short-term annoyance compared with the single-vehicle baseline, showing that AVAS optimisation should consider scene-level interactions in addition to individual-source detectability.

talks.title: Frequency-Modulated and Single-Tone Excitation to Reveal Vibro-Acoustic Nonlinearities in Loosened Bolted Joints
talks.authors: Berkay Kullukçu, R. Pianowski, and D. Hannebauer
talks.abstract: Loss of preload changes the stiffness, damping, and nonlinear response of a bolted joint. A laboratory demonstrator was investigated at several preload levels using a triaxial accelerometer and a microphone under single-tone and frequency-modulated excitation. Harmonic content and additional spectral components were evaluated as vibro-acoustic indicators for distinguishing loosened from highly preloaded states.

talks.title: SIAMIS: Experimental Study on the Acoustic Behavior of Bolted Joints Under Varying Tightening Torques
talks.authors: Berkay Kullukçu, R. Kamenzky, and D. Hannebauer
talks.abstract: Controlled impact tests were used to investigate the airborne and structure-borne acoustic responses of bolted specimens at different tightening torques. Microphone and accelerometer measurements were evaluated through transfer functions, resonance shifts, damping-related quantities, and sound-pressure trends. The study identifies preload-sensitive features that can support acoustic and vibration-based condition monitoring of bolted joints.

talks.title: Psychoacoustic Modelling of AVAS Sounds: Consumer-Centric Semantic Attribute Development for Electric Vehicles
talks.authors: Berkay Kullukçu, M. Engel, Serkan Atamer, and M. Ercan Altinsoy
talks.abstract: The work develops consumer-centred semantic descriptors for Acoustic Vehicle Alerting System sounds and connects them to measurable psychoacoustic properties. AVAS samples were evaluated using semantic differential scales, and statistical modelling was used to determine which perceptual attributes distinguish preferred and non-preferred sounds. The resulting vocabulary supports the translation of user expectations into engineering-oriented sound-design targets.

talks.title: Experimental Determination of the Masking Threshold in Electric Trucks
talks.authors: Berkay Kullukçu, Oliver Zeidler, Serkan Atamer, and M. Ercan Altinsoy
talks.abstract: This study examines how the interior noise of electric trucks masks tonal auditory cues. Binaural recordings at different driving speeds were reproduced under controlled laboratory conditions, and frequency-dependent detection thresholds were measured in listening tests. The results provide a basis for designing in-cabin warning sounds that remain detectable without becoming unnecessarily intrusive.

talks.title: Designing Effective Warning Sounds: Psychoacoustical Considerations
talks.authors: Berkay Kullukçu, Jin Wook Lee, Serkan Atamer, and M. Ercan Altinsoy
talks.abstract: Warning sounds must attract attention and communicate urgency while avoiding excessive annoyance. Two listening-test studies examined how psychoacoustic characteristics influence perceived urgency and annoyance. The presentation discusses how temporal and spectral design parameters can be combined to create warning signals that are effective, interpretable, and acceptable to listeners.

talks.title: Acoustic Vehicle Alerting Systems (AVAS) for Electric Trucks: Initial Thoughts for the Requirements
talks.authors: Berkay Kullukçu, Robert Rosenkranz, and M. Ercan Altinsoy
talks.abstract: A semantic-differential listening study compared internal-combustion and electric cars and trucks, both with and without AVAS. The analysis identified perceptual differences such as truck-likeness, effortlessness, whistling, and howling, and related these attributes to objective psychoacoustic properties. The findings form an initial requirements framework for designing electric-truck AVAS that communicate vehicle size and character without excessive annoyance.
